title: Git Fundamentals
description:
## Git: A Distributed Version Control System

**Git** tracks changes to files over time so you can review history, collaborate, and undo mistakes. Unlike older centralized systems, Git is **distributed**: every clone is a full copy of the repository — complete history included — so you can commit, branch, and inspect the log entirely offline.

### The three areas

Work in Git flows between three places:

| Area | What it holds |
|---|---|
| **Working directory** | The actual files you edit on disk. |
| **Staging area (index)** | A snapshot you've marked to include in the *next* commit. `git add` moves changes here. |
| **Local repository** | The committed history — the `.git` directory. `git commit` records the staged snapshot here. |

```
  working dir  --git add-->  staging area  --git commit-->  local repo
```

### The core flow

```bash
git init                 # create a new repository
git status               # see what changed and what is staged
git add file.txt         # stage a change (git add . stages everything)
git commit -m "message"  # record the staged snapshot in history
git log --oneline        # view the commit history
```

### Remotes

A **remote** is a copy of the repository hosted elsewhere (e.g. on GitHub). The default remote is conventionally named **`origin`**. You synchronize with it using `push` and `pull`:

```bash
git clone <url>          # copy a remote repo locally (sets up origin)
git remote -v            # list configured remotes
git push origin main     # upload your local commits to the remote
git pull origin main     # fetch remote commits and merge them in
```

### Branches

A **branch** is a movable pointer to a commit. Branches let you develop features in isolation without disturbing the main line of work (commonly `main`).

```bash
git branch                       # list branches
git switch -c feature/login      # create and switch to a new branch
git switch main                  # switch back
git merge feature/login          # integrate the branch into main
```

### Mental model

> A commit is an immutable **snapshot** of your whole project plus a pointer to its parent. Branches and tags are just lightweight pointers *to* commits.

Once comfortable with `add` → `commit` → `push`/`pull` and branching, you have the foundation every DevOps workflow builds on.
